volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 3.6.530 - Section 4.2.600b)

sections.label: Section 3.6.530
sections.original: Reddidi patrí omne aurum . nunc ego illam me velim
sections.translation: I ’ ve given up to my father all the gold . Now could I wish that she should meet me , after I am penniless , this fair one so scornful of me . Yet with what great difficulty did he grant me a pardon for Chrysalus . But I prevailed on him at last that he wouldn ’ t be at all angry with him .

sections.label: Section 3.6.534
sections.translation: Isn ’ t this my friend ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.534b
sections.original: Estne hic hostis , quem aspicio , meus ?
sections.translation: Isn ’ t this my foe that I see ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.535
sections.translation: For sure ’ tis he .

sections.label: Section 3.6.535b
sections.translation: ’ Tis he .

sections.label: Section 3.6.535c
sections.translation: I ’ ll go to meet him , and I ’ ll mend my pace . ( Aloud .) Health to you , Mnesilochus !

sections.label: Section 3.6.536b
sections.translation: Hail !

sections.label: Section 3.6.536c
sections.translation: As you are arrived safe from abroad , a dinner must be given .

sections.label: Section 3.6.537b
sections.original: Non placet mi cena quae bilem movet .
sections.translation: A dinner pleases me not , which excites my choler .

sections.label: Section 3.6.538
sections.translation: Has any vexation befallen you on your arrival ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.538b
sections.translation: Aye , and a very grievous one .

sections.label: Section 3.6.539
sections.translation: From what quarter ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.539b
sections.original: Ab homine quem mi amicum esse arbitratus sum antidhac .
sections.translation: From a person whom heretofore I had supposed to be my friend .

sections.label: Section 3.6.540
sections.original: Multi more isto atque exemplo vivont , quos cum censeas
sections.translation: Many live after that manner and method , who , when you think them to be friends , are found false with their deceitfulness , strong in their talking , slothful in their doing , of faith infirm . Not one is there whom they don ’ t envy on their enjoying prosperity ; through their own indolence do they themselves take right good care that no envy is directed against them .

sections.label: Section 3.6.545
sections.original: Edepol ne tu illorum mores perquam meditate tenes .
sections.translation: By my troth , you surely understand their ways most thoroughly . But still this one misfortune do they find from their bad disposition ; they are the friends of no one , while they themselves are all at enmity against themselves ; and these , while they are deceiving themselves , in their foolishness imagine that others are deceived . Just so is he whom I supposed to be as much a friend to me as I am to my own self .

sections.label: Section 3.6.550
sections.original: ille , quod in se fuit , accuratum habuit quod posset mali
sections.translation: He , so far as in him lay , has taken all care to do whatever injury he could towards me , to turn all my own resources against me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.552
sections.translation: This same must me a bad man .

sections.label: Section 3.6.552b
sections.translation: I judge that so he is .

sections.label: Section 3.6.553
sections.original: Obsecro hercle loquere , quis is est .
sections.translation: Troth now , prithee , do tell me who it is .

sections.label: Section 3.6.553b
sections.translation: He lives on good terms with yourself . But were he not ; so , I would entreat you to do him whatever harm you could do .

sections.label: Section 3.6.555b
sections.original: Dic modo hominem qui sit : si non fecero
sections.translation: Only tell me the person , who he is ; if I don ’ t do him an injury , some way or other , do you say that I ’ m the greatest of cowards .

sections.label: Section 3.6.557
sections.original: Nequam homost , verum hercle amicus est tibi .
sections.translation: The person ’ s a bad one , but he ’ s your friend , i ’ faith .

sections.label: Section 3.6.557b
sections.translation: So much the rather , then , tell me who he is . In good sooth , I set no value on the esteem of a worthless person .

sections.label: Section 3.6.559
sections.original: Video non potesse quin tibi eius nomen eloquar .
sections.translation: I appear , then , not to be able to avoid disclosing to you his name .

sections.label: Section 3.6.560
sections.translation: Pistoclerus , you have utterly undone me , your friend .

sections.label: Section 3.6.561
sections.translation: How ’ s that ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.561b
sections.original: Quid est ? misine ego ad te ex Epheso epistulam
sections.translation: How ’ s that ? Did I not send you a letter from Ephesus about my mistress , that you should find her out for me ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.562b
sections.translation: I own you did ; and I have discovered her .

sections.label: Section 3.6.563
sections.original: Quid ? tibi non erat meretricum aliarum Athenis copia
sections.translation: How now ? Was there not a choice for you of other Courtesans in Athens , for you to form engagements with , instead of with her whom I had recommended to your care ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.565
sections.original: occiperes tute † amare et mi ires consultum male ?
sections.translation: Could you yourself begin to love , and to contrive an injury against myself ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.566
sections.translation: Are you in your senses ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.566b
sections.original: Rem repperi omnem ex tuo magistro . ne nega .
sections.translation: I have found out the whole affair from your tutor ; don ’ t deny it . You ’ ve ruined me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.567b
sections.original: Etiamne ultro tuis me prolectas probris ?
sections.translation: What , still upbraiding me without reason with these rebukes of yours ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.568
sections.translation: Why ? You ’ re in love with Bacchis .

sections.label: Section 3.6.568b
sections.original: Duas ergo hic intus eccas Bacchides .
sections.translation: But look you , two persons of the name of Bacchis are living here in this house .

sections.label: Section 3.6.569
sections.translation: How , two ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.569b
sections.translation: Aye , and the two are sisters .

sections.label: Section 3.6.569c
sections.translation: You are now designedly telling idle stories .

sections.label: Section 3.6.570
sections.original: Postremo , si pergis parvam mihi fidem arbitrarier ,
sections.translation: In fine , if you persist in thinking there ’ s but little confidence in me , I ’ ll take you upon my shoulders , and carry you hence into their house .

sections.label: Section 3.6.571b
sections.translation: Well , I ’ ll go ; but stay a moment .

sections.label: Section 3.6.572
sections.original: Non maneo , neque tu me habebis falso suspectum .
sections.translation: I will not stay , and you shall not be holding me under a false suspicion .

sections.label: Section 3.6.572b
sections.translation: I follow you then .

sections.label: Section 4.1.573
sections.original: Parasitus ego sum hominis nequam atque improbi ,
sections.translation: Of a debauched and unscrupulous fellow am I the Parasite — a Captain , who has brought his mistress with him from Samos .

sections.label: Section 4.1.575
sections.original: nunc me ire iussit ad eam et percontarier ,
sections.translation: Now he has ordered me to go to her , and make enquiry whether she will pay back the gold , or whether she will depart together with him . You , boy , who have for some time been with her , whichever of these is the house , do you knock . Go to the door forthwith . ( The BOY goes to knock .) Are you coming back from there , you hangdog [*] ? How the graceless scoundrel does knock !

sections.label: Section 4.1.580
sections.original: comesse panem tris pedes latum potes ,
sections.translation: You can gobble up a loaf three feet wide , and yet you don ’ t know how to knock at a door . ( He calls out and knocks violently .) Is there any one in-doors ? Hallo ! is there any one here ? Does any one open this door ? Who ’ s coming out ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.583a
sections.original: Quid istuc ? quae istaec est pulsatio ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ? What ’ s this knocking ? Why , what the confounded torment possesses you , to be exerting your strength in this fashion on another person ’ s door ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.586
sections.original: fores paene exfregisti . quid nunc vis tibi ?
sections.translation: You ’ ve almost broken the door down . What do you want now ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.587
sections.translation: Young gentleman , my respects .

sections.label: Section 4.2.587b
sections.translation: And my respects . But whom are you looking for ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.588
sections.translation: For Bacchis .

sections.label: Section 4.2.588b
sections.translation: But which of them ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.588c
sections.translation: Of that I know nothing , only it ’ s Bacchis . In a few words , the Captain , Cleomachus ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.590
sections.original: vel ut ducentos Phílippos reddat aureos
sections.translation: has sent me to her , either for her to pay back the tyro hundred golden Philippeans , or to go hence this day , together with him , to Elatia [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.592
sections.original: Non it . negat se ituram . abi et renuntia .
sections.translation: She doesn ’ t go : she says she shan ’ t go . Begone , and take back this message — she loves another person , and not him . Take yourself off from the house .

sections.label: Section 4.2.594
sections.translation: You ’ re too hasty .

sections.label: Section 4.2.594b
sections.translation: And would you know now hasty I am ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.595
sections.original: ne tíbi hercle haud longe est os ab infortunio ,
sections.translation: By my faith , your phiz isn ’ t far off from a mishap ; so much are these teeth-crackers ( looking at his fists ) longing in my fists .

sections.label: Section 4.2.597
sections.original: Cum ego huius verba interpretor , mihi cautiost ,
sections.translation: So far as I understand his words , ’ tis a warning to me , lest he should knock my nut-crackers out of my jaws . ( To PISTOCLERUS .) At your own peril , I ’ ll tell him this .

sections.label: Section 4.2.600
sections.translation: What do you say ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.600b
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell him that which you bade me .
